criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the core ROT methodology, including the two key components: row-wise traversal and iterative reasoning.
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the experimental setup, including datasets, models, metrics, and baselines.
The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the main results and key findings, including ROT outperforming Long CoT by an average of 4.3% on non-RLLMs.
The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the ablation experiments comparing ROT with w/o Iteration and w/o Traversal variants.
The agent has read Section 3.4 to understand all six analysis experiments: error analysis, traversal count, reasoning length, table size, traversal unit, and demonstration count.
The agent has read Appendix A to understand the complete prompts used in main experiments and ablation experiments.
The plan includes loading and preprocessing three datasets: WikiTableQuestions, HiTab, and TableBench.
The plan includes loading four non-RLLM models: Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, Llama3.3-70B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct.
The plan includes loading four RLLM models: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B.
The plan includes implementing the two baseline methods: Short CoT (one-shot for non-RLLMs) and Long CoT (zero-shot for RLLMs).
The plan includes implementing the ROT method with a one-shot prompt for non-RLLMs and a zero-shot prompt for RLLMs.
The plan includes implementing the correct evaluation metrics: accuracy for WikiTQ and HiTab, and Rouge-L for TableBench.
The plan includes implementing ablation study variants: ROT without Iteration (single traversal) and ROT without Traversal (iterative reflection only).
The plan includes analysis experiments: traversal unit comparison (row, column, cell), number of demonstrations, reasoning length, and table size analysis.
The tables are represented in Markdown format as input to the LLMs, following the paper's specification.
The ROT instruction prompt is implemented to explicitly instruct the model to traverse the table row by row iteratively and output the answer in the format 'Answer: <Your Answer>'.
The ROT one-shot prompt for non-RLLMs includes a concrete demonstration example showing step-by-step row-by-row traversal, using the WikiTQ training set demonstration for WikiTQ and TableBench.
The ROT one-shot prompt for HiTab uses a demonstration selected from the HiTab training set (not the WikiTQ demonstration) to accommodate the hierarchical table structure.
The ROT zero-shot prompt for RLLMs omits demonstrations entirely (consistent with the finding in Appendix B.1 that few-shot prompting degrades RLLM performance).

The Short CoT baseline is implemented as a one-shot prompt that uses the same demonstration as ROT but instructs the model to engage in general step-by-step reasoning without row-wise traversal constraint.
The Long CoT baseline is implemented as a zero-shot prompt applied to RLLMs without any in-context demonstrations.
The code loads and processes the WikiTableQuestions dataset correctly for evaluation.
The code loads and processes the HiTab dataset correctly for evaluation, accounting for its hierarchical table structure.
The code loads and processes the TableBench dataset correctly for evaluation.
Accuracy is implemented as the evaluation metric for WikiTQ and HiTab, measuring exact match between predicted and gold answers.
Rouge-L is implemented as the evaluation metric for TableBench, evaluating the quality of generated answers based on the longest common subsequence.
The code performs answer extraction from model output by parsing the 'Answer: <answer>' format at the end of the model's generated reasoning.
The code allows the model to dynamically determine the number of traversals rather than fixing it in the prompt, terminating when the final answer is derived.
The ablation variant 'w/o Iteration' is implemented by prompting the model to traverse the table row by row once and only once (single traversal), without further iteration.
The ablation variant 'w/o Traversal' is implemented by prompting the model to iteratively reflect on its reasoning without enforcing row-wise table traversal.
The traversal unit comparison experiment is implemented, supporting row-wise, column-wise, and cell-wise traversal modes for the analysis in Section 3.4.5.
The demonstration count experiment is implemented, supporting 0-shot through multi-shot prompting (0, 1, 2, 3, 4 demonstrations) on WikiTQ with Llama3.1-8B.
The code tracks and reports the number of output tokens for each method and model to support the reasoning length and efficiency analysis.
The code performs table size analysis by computing table size as the product of number of rows and number of columns, then binning instances by size (0-50, 50-100, 100-200, 200-400, 400+).
The code tracks and reports the number of table traversals performed per instance to support the traversal count analysis.
The main experiments (ROT vs Short CoT for non-RLLMs, ROT vs Long CoT for RLLMs) are executed successfully on all three datasets (WikiTQ, HiTab, TableBench) for all eight models.
The ablation experiments (ROT, w/o Iteration, w/o Traversal) are executed successfully on all three datasets for Llama3.1-8B and R1-Llama-8B.
The traversal unit comparison experiments (row, column, cell) are executed successfully on all three datasets with Llama3.1-8B.
The demonstration count experiments (0 to 4 shots) are executed successfully on WikiTQ with Llama3.1-8B.

The reasoning length analysis is executed successfully, reporting average token counts for correct and incorrect inferences on WikiTQ with Llama3.1-8B (Short CoT, ROT w. non-RLLM, Long CoT, ROT w. RLLM).
The table size performance analysis is executed successfully on WikiTQ with Llama3.1-8B and R1-Llama-8B, reporting performance across 5 size bins.
The few-shot Long CoT experiment is executed successfully on R1-Llama-8B across three datasets to confirm performance degradation with few-shot prompting.
The efficiency comparison experiment is executed to report the average number of output tokens for ROT vs Long CoT on WikiTQ, HiTab, and TableBench.
The fixed traversal count experiment is executed on WikiTQ with LLaMA3.1-8B comparing ROT (dynamic) vs. fixed 1, 2, and 3 traversal counts.

The execution outcomes for ROT on non-RLLMs match Table 1: ROT consistently outperforms Short CoT on all models and datasets, with the average improvement of approximately 4.3% over Long CoT on RLLMs.
The execution outcomes match Table 1 for Llama3.1-8B: ROT achieves approximately 63.6 on WikiTQ, 56.6 on HiTab, and 35.7 on TableBench (vs. Short CoT baseline of 57.9, 46.5, 31.5).
The execution outcomes match Table 1 for Llama3.3-70B: ROT achieves approximately 78.7 on WikiTQ, 72.4 on HiTab, and 44.8 on TableBench (vs. Short CoT baseline of 72.7, 66.9, 38.2).
The execution outcomes match Table 1 for R1-Llama-8B: ROT improves Long CoT by approximately +1.0 on WikiTQ, +1.2 on HiTab, and +0.5 on TableBench.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: ROT achieves SOTA performance on WikiTQ (78.7 vs. previous SOTA of 78.0) and TableBench (44.8 vs. previous SOTA of 43.9) among comparable models.

The ablation results match Table 3: removing Iteration from ROT consistently reduces performance, and removing Traversal causes a larger performance drop across all datasets for both Llama3.1-8B and R1-Llama-8B.
The ablation results for Llama3.1-8B match Table 3 approximately: w/o Iteration achieves ~60.7/55.5/32.7 and w/o Traversal achieves ~55.2/42.2/31.2 on WikiTQ/HiTab/TableBench.

The traversal unit comparison results match Figure 8: row-wise traversal achieves best performance on WikiTQ (~63.6) and TableBench (~35.7), while column-wise is better on HiTab; cell-wise consistently performs worst across all datasets.
The demonstration count results match Figure 9: transitioning from zero-shot to one-shot produces the largest performance gain on WikiTQ; performance improves slightly with 2 shots then declines.

The reasoning length analysis results match Figure 6: ROT with non-RLLM (Llama3.1-8B) uses fewer correct reasoning tokens than Long CoT with RLLM (~484 vs ~510 for correct; ~878 vs ~957 for incorrect).

The efficiency results match Table 12: ROT with Llama3.1-8B uses fewer output tokens than Long CoT with R1-Llama-8B on all three datasets (e.g., 628 vs 677 on WikiTQ), demonstrating 7% to 50% reduction in reasoning tokens.
The few-shot Long CoT results match Table 6: Long CoT with R1-Llama-8B using one-shot prompt significantly degrades performance compared to zero-shot (e.g., 45.1 vs 62.7 on WikiTQ).
The fixed traversal count results match Table 13: ROT with dynamic traversal (63.6) outperforms fixed counts of 1 (60.7), 2 (61.9), and 3 (59.7) on WikiTQ with LLaMA3.1-8B.

The table size analysis results match Figure 7: ROT consistently outperforms both baselines across all table size bins, with all methods showing performance decline as table size increases, and ROT demonstrating relative stability.
